Datable Vedic passages  It was observed that the moon came back to the same position in the zodiac once in about 27 days and that each day was marked by the asterism or asterismal group (naksatra) near which the moon was seen, resulting in calling the asterism as the day’s nak

chapters.title: Text of the Vedinga Jyotisa
chapters.content_preview: 3. Text of the Vedinga Jyotisa  Of the extant Indian astronomical texts, the VJ is the earliest. The astronomical matter forming the basis of the work is of one Lagadha, but the classical language employed in the work as current now would indicate that the original must have been

chapters.title: Date of the Vedanga Jyotisa
chapters.content_preview: 4. Date of the Vedanga Jyotisa  Verses 6, 7 and 8 of the Yajur-Vedanga Jyotisa (Y-VJ) show that at the time of Lagadha the winter solstice was at the beginning of the asterism Sravisthad (Delphini) segment and that the summer solstice was at the mid-point of the Aslesd segment. Lessen this by 1, multiply by 12, again multiply by 2, add the parvas gone in

chapters.title: VEDANGA JYOTISA
chapters.content_preview: 52 VEDANGA JYOTISA  Anuvatsara is the fourth year of the yuga. So, 3 years, 9 months and one parva (Sukla) are gone. From this:  Parva-rai=-(3X 12+9) 2+1-+2 (for 60 parvas gone)=93  Note 1, In each year there are 12 synodic months, each month having 2 parvas.
